item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article has been translated into Spanish
http://lafundacionscp.wikidot.com/scp-5029

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha yeah lol. They kinda of are my thing! I would like to try my hand at anomalies similar to this with motives that are different though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had just finished reading this when I said to myself "this seems like something DianaBerry would write."

Clicked onto the discussion page, and … yeah.

Seems like moderately dangerous objects with benevolent motives are kind of your shtick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yooooo yesssssss

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: omg now we gotta write that tale

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, I didn't even think of that lol! Now I want to draw Miss J and girl.png together!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The timeline is absolutely backwards, but.. this could be the start of SCP-5094. Similar, but different enough. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: AWWWW THIS IS SO CUTE! I adore this! :D Good job! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
BTW….

Did a Quick Drawing Version of SCP-5209
Humanized, featuring my SCP OC, Esther P.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Glad you like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reformed computer virus who has made art a skill to educate for others.

I like it

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see where you’re coming from. The story was meant to be short and sweet, but I understand that I could have done that in a way that didn’t also make it feel rushed. Thank you for your comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could have sworn I hit reply to Gee but apparently I didn’t, but this is meant as a reply to Gee

Then, in only a couple of paragraphs, the anomaly both discovers it's damaging files and then fixes it, with no actual struggle or conflict apparent at all beyond a couple of sad emoticons. This feels pretty rushed and undeveloped and just leaves me disappointed at how the story played out, since the actual events are over and done with so much faster than the scene-setting interviews.

I was concerned about that, but after a few reviews I concluded that it would be fine, which I suppose is my bad. I want to expand upon the emotions a bit, I wasn’t entirely sure how to carry it out in a way that wouldn’t be dragging things out. In a very rough draft of this, I had another interview after an incident log, but I was afraid that it was too similar to other AI SCPs, so I decided to go a bit of a different route. Thank you for comment, I completely understand where you’re coming from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I understand your concerns, and now that it’s pointed out to me, I see that you’re right. I’m almost tempted to add in those lines you suggested as I feel they would greatly help, but I don’t want to steal.

Also, for why he’s telling the anomaly about himself, who said he was telling the truth? Foundation personnel often have to put on a show for the purpose of research. I suppose I should have made this more clear, I might go in and add a line to clarify this.

Given this information, why would the Foundation try interacting with the virus in the first place?

I suppose I was just thinking well, for the purpose of research.

I also don't see why the Foundation might be so friendly with it in the first place when it's already shown causing widespread damage to people's devices.

Personally, I don’t think the Foundation is always nothing but cold and strict. This virus has the mind of a child, it wouldn’t understand harshness. Just like how the Foundation is generally softer to child humanoids, they’re softer here.

I appreciate your critique, and your points are very valid. I’ll see what I can do about trying to make the Foundation portrayal more realistic. Thank you for your comment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with Gee here. I could go on about the varying faults of it but chief among them is that it is simply rushed and boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Flops' comments about the realism of this article make good points, but I've got a different problem. Namely that the actual story arc here is really not remotely interesting:

There's initially two logs of the researcher and anomaly interacting, which only really serve to establish how exactly the anomaly sees itself and its personality (which generally seems to be 'annoying', as the intrusive childlike typing is the only unique personality trait that stands out). Right at the end of these logs, the researcher mentions that the anomaly is harming files, but it's largely glossed over.

Then, in only a couple of paragraphs, the anomaly both discovers it's damaging files and then fixes it, with no actual struggle or conflict apparent at all beyond a couple of sad emoticons. This feels pretty rushed and undeveloped and just leaves me disappointed at how the story played out, since the actual events are over and done with so much faster than the scene-setting interviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having some trouble with this one.

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-5029 is capable of self-downloading onto devices. SCP-5029 cannot be downloaded from any application stores, and will not appear if searched.

How does it find devices to infect? What is its vector? Or is it floating freely, as it were, through the Internet? If the Foundation knows where it ended up, couldn't they also technically learn where and when it first appeared, and then, whomever disseminated it?

These questions don't necessarily need to be brought up, but when basic questions aren't addressed in the article proper, I'm left confused about how it can even operate. I think even a simple "SCP-5029's means of travel are as-yet-undetermined" wording could rectify this and quell any questions. But right now, it seems the Foundation is unconcerned where it came from or how it can travel in that way, despite it being wildly hazardous.

Once opened, SCP-5029 cannot be closed until an interaction with SCP-5029-1 is made. After closure, all files and applications on the affected device will revert to an unfinished state. Instances of files and applications being deleted completely have also been noted.

Given this information, why would the Foundation try interacting with the virus in the first place? This is essentially ransomware, except there's no "key", and no evident "ransom" that motivated its creator to cut it loose. Surely, the Foundation wouldn't, as even basic interaction poses a considerable security and privacy risk — not just for their own employees, but the Foundation network at large. Again, a simple "SCP-5029 is currently undergoing study via non-Internet-capable secure devices/virtual machines" would work to alleviate this question. The early mention of

The internet servers of Site-90 have been rerouted […]

— and later mention of —

[…] outside of its intended testing range.

— don't, because at the same time, it shows the Foundation offering an unnecessary amount of leeway. I do understand this was done, narratively, so that its personality can be known, but the portrayal of its personality also left me discouraged. The article also shows the application brute-forcing into Foundation servers anyway, as though it will merely get what it wants regardless of what the Foundation does — so, again, why offer it the leeway?

Also:

Why is Researcher Cherri letting the anomaly know so much about him? Doesn't that also pose a considerable security risk? It almost appears as though Cherri's intervention here is the reason it spread into Foundation servers, and there is no mention of Cherri having expertise in this field — so why was he assigned?

Personally, until Addendum 3, the Foundation feels either incompetent here, or dangerously careless. This is a highly infectious computer virus that travels independent of any initial source, seemingly between numerous infected computers without a known vector, and even somehow accesses reality outside the computer so as to see into people's homes, offices, and Foundation sites. And because it can access reality, isn't letting the anomaly know so much about Cherri also an unintended risk?

I will note that I'm definitely not a computer expert, but to me, the way it works and the way the Foundation approaches it aren't sensical. I also don't see why the Foundation might be so friendly with it in the first place when it's already shown causing widespread damage to people's devices.

I do see the amount of work and effort put into this article, but it doesn't work for me.

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, thanks for pointing that out to me! And I’m glad you liked it! Yeah I understand your feelings there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what to feel about the anomaly. I did like the article, it's funny, but it's kind of creepy at the same time. Anyway, you got an upvote. By the way, I found a tiny punctuation mistake in one of the sentences.

Hm. My pets, my family, food. I wasn't a very creative child, but hey, I tried

You just forgot about adding a point at the end of the sentence.

Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah! Yeah I meant to shorten that but I couldn't find a way to do it, I'll look into it again. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it! My one thought would be that there is a sentence in the "Discovery" section that's kinda long and I had a hard time following it, but that could be easily fixed by rewording or turning it into a few sentences!
"SCP-5029 first came to the Foundation's attention when word of a computer virus that destroys data by simplifying it that can download itself onto devices through unknown means began to spread."
Either way, +1

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
Hello! I honestly love writing SCPs, so I'm glad an idea finally came to me. Fun fact, I drew girl.png's original design on my phone while I was half asleep. Fun, huh?

Anyways, I hope you enjoy this sweet story about a computer virus with the mind of a child that just likes to help people. I was trying to capture the nature of how sometimes kids will destroy things thinking their helping, when in reality they aren't. I hope I managed to do that!

 Spoilers
- Close

Oh also, the application being called a png and such when it isn't is on purpose. She's a virus. She can't make a proper application.

Thank you so much to my long list of critters! I wouldn't have made this without you guys!

 Crit credit
- Close

MalyceGraves— Helped with direction and story content.
Greyve— Helped review in its early stages.
KaraKatt— Helped with overall review and gave suggestions on how to flesh out the child-like nature a bit more.
chiifu— Helped with review in general and helped make sure the flow was right. Also, helped me phrase this line= 'it isn't random! nevr random! everywon needs help!'
Flopmind— Helped with SPAG and rephrasing and overall review and such.
jimmyjimjim— Helped with critique and review in general.
BlueJones— Reviewed in general.
Dr Moned— Helped review and general and helped with logistics.
PlaguePJP- Helped with SPAG and phrasing and sentence flow. Also, phrased this line: 'Do you know that you have powers that not all other computer applications have?', this line: 'Upon initialization of the antivirus software, the following message was found of SCP-XXXX infected devices', and this line: 'Researcher Cherri noted that he had installed the antivirus software created by the Foundation'. Also suggested I focus more on the data destroying aspect.
Meska— Helped review the first addendum, gave me suggestions on how to fix it up. Also, rephrased this sentence: 'Research into ways to undo the damage caused by SCP-X is still ongoing.'
